1: Theme. After twenty years of building — the house of YHWH and his own house — Solomon turns to the cities Hiram had given back. The completion of the temple project marks a transition: from concentrated building to expansive reign. Christ, having completed the work of salvation (“It is finished”), now reigns and extends his kingdom through the proclamation of the gospel. The post-completion expansion of Solomon’s realm shadows the post-resurrection expansion of Christ’s kingdom through the church (Acts 1:8).
2: Theme. Solomon resettled the cities Hiram returned and put the people of Israel in them — the repopulation of returned cities. The returning of what was given as partial payment and its repopulation with God’s people prefigures the reclamation of the nations for God: what was “given” in the Fall is reclaimed and repopulated with the people of the new covenant (2 Corinthians 5:19, “God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself”).
3: Theme. Solomon went to Hamath-zobah and took it — military conquest expanding the Solomonic realm to its Davidic maximum. The promise of Psalms 2:8 (“ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage”) is what Solomon partially fulfills. Christ receives this inheritance fully: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me” (Matthew 28:18), and his kingdom expands not through military force but through the Spirit-empowered proclamation of the gospel (Romans 15:19-20).
4: Theme. Solomon built Tadmor (Palmyra) in the wilderness and storage cities in Hamath — establishing infrastructure in distant territories. Christ builds his church in the “wilderness” of the nations, establishing communities of the redeemed in places far from Jerusalem. The storage cities that provision the kingdom correspond to the local churches that store and distribute the spiritual resources of Christ’s kingdom (Ephesians 4:11-16).
5: Theme. Solomon built Upper and Lower Beth-horon as fortified cities — securing the strategic passes into the land. Christ secures the entry into the eternal city: “I am the door; if anyone enters through me he will be saved” (John 10:9). The fortified approaches that prevented enemy penetration prefigure Christ’s promise that “the gates of Hades will not prevail” against his church (Matthew 16:18). He both provides access (the door) and prevents enemy access (the fortified gate).
6: Theme. Baalath, all of Solomon’s storage cities, chariot cities, cavalry cities — the infrastructure of the greatest earthly kingdom. In Christ “all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell” (Colossians 1:19) and “in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3). The storehouses and supply systems of Solomon’s empire are a shadow of the inexhaustible resources of Christ’s kingdom — “the riches of his grace, which he lavished upon us” (Ephesians 1:7-8).
7: Shadow. The non-Israelite peoples remaining in the land — Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — not destroyed but made into forced laborers. The survival of Canaanite peoples within Solomon’s kingdom and their service to it prefigures the Gentile inclusion in God’s people. Those who were once enemies of Israel (Ephesians 2:12, “alienated from the commonwealth of Israel”) are incorporated into the household of God through Christ. Former opponents become “fellow heirs, members of the same body, and partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus” (Ephesians 3:6).
8: Shadow. The descendants of those peoples whom Israel had not destroyed were made into a levy of forced workers — a status that continued to the time of the Chronicler. The nations serving the Davidic king is the shadow of the nations serving the greater David. But the labor is transformed: not compelled service to a human empire but willing worship to the risen King (“every knee will bow” — Philippians 2:10-11), motivated not by military subjugation but by the gospel of redeeming love.
9: Shadow. “But of the people of Israel Solomon made no slaves for his work; they were soldiers, commanders of his officers, and commanders of his chariots and cavalry.” Israel serves as leaders and warriors, not as laborers. The people of God under the new covenant are described as “a royal priesthood” (1 Peter 2:9) and “priests and kings” (Revelation 1:6; 2 Chronicles 5:10) — not slaves but sons, not servants of the earthly machinery but participants in the royal and priestly governance of the new creation. Christ frees his people from slavery to make them co-heirs of the kingdom (Galatians 4:7; Romans 8:17).
10: Shadow. The 250 chief officers who exercised authority over the people under Solomon — the administrative hierarchy of the kingdom. Christ appoints his own officers for the governance of his kingdom: apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, and teachers (Ephesians 4:11), given “to equip the saints for the work of ministry”. The twelve apostles will sit on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel in the age to come (Matthew 19:28) — the administrative pattern of Solomon’s kingdom fulfilled in an eternal judicial-governing role.
11: Theme. Solomon moved Pharaoh’s daughter from the City of David to the house he built for her, because “the places are holy where the ark of YHWH has come.” The maintenance of holiness boundaries — not excluding the Egyptian princess from the kingdom, but distinguishing what belongs to the holy precincts. Christ maintains the holy character of the new Jerusalem: “nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false, but only those who are written in the Lamb’s book of life” (Revelation 21:27). Holiness is not the rejection of the Gentile but the transformation required to dwell in the divine presence.
12: Shadow. Solomon offered burnt offerings on the altar — daily, Sabbath, new moon, and festival offerings. The regular rhythm of Solomonic sacrifice points beyond itself. Christ fulfilled the entire sacrificial calendar: he is the Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7), the firstfruits of the resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20-23, at the Feast of Firstfruits), and the source of the Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2). The “daily” rhythm of sacrifice anticipates the once-for-all sacrifice that makes all further repetition unnecessary (Hebrews 10:10).
13: Shadow. The three annual feasts — Unleavened Bread (Passover), Weeks (Pentecost), and Tabernacles — the complete cycle of Israel’s festival calendar. Paul writes that these festivals are “a shadow of the things to come, but the substance belongs to Christ” (Colossians 2:16-17). Christ is the reality that each feast was designed to anticipate: the Passover Lamb, the firstfruits of the Spirit (Pentecost), and the final harvest-ingathering of all nations (Tabernacles/Revelation 7:9). The entire festival calendar is a curriculum in Christ.
14: Shadow. “He appointed the divisions of the priests for their service, and the Levites for their posts of praise and ministry before the priests as the duty of each day required.” The carefully maintained Davidic order — priests and Levites in their assignments, functioning without deviation. Hebrews applies the priestly pattern to Christ: he is the great high priest in the order of Melchizedek (Hebrews 7:11-17) whose priesthood is “inviolable because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24). The organized priestly service that Solomon maintained points to the eternal, unchanging priestly ministry of Christ.
15: Theme. “They did not deviate from the king’s commandment to the priests and Levites in any matter, or in the matter of the treasuries.” Perfect obedience to royal command — the Solomonic organization executed without deviation. Christ’s servants under the new covenant are called to similar faithfulness: “it is required of stewards that they be found faithful” (1 Corinthians 4:2). But more fundamentally, Christ himself is the servant who deviated from God’s command in nothing: “he was obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8) — the perfect execution of the Father’s royal command.
16: Theme. “Thus was accomplished all the work of Solomon from the day the foundation was laid until it was finished. So the house of YHWH was completed.” The completion summary — foundation to finish. Christ is both the foundation (“no one can lay a foundation other than the one that is laid, which is Jesus Christ,” 1 Corinthians 3:11) and the one who brings the work to completion. His “It is finished” (John 19:30) corresponds to this verse’s completed-work summary: what Solomon completed in stone and cedar, Christ completed in flesh and blood, and the completion is eternal.
17: Theme. Solomon went to Ezion-geber and Eloth on the Red Sea — the southernmost reach of his commercial network, the gateway to the Indian Ocean trade routes. The expansion to the sea’s edge shadows the missional reach of the gospel to “the ends of the earth” (Acts 1:8; Isaiah 49:6). Solomon’s trading missions that bring the wealth of distant peoples to Jerusalem prefigure the gathering of all peoples into the new Jerusalem, “the kings of the earth bringing their glory and honor into it” (Revelation 21:24-26).
18: Shadow. Hiram sends Solomon ships and experienced sailors; they bring 450 talents of gold from Ophir. The nations’ wealth flowing to Solomon’s kingdom is the shadow of the eschatological ingathering prophesied in Psalms 72:10-11 (“May the kings of Tarshish and of the coastlands render him tribute”) and Revelation 21:24-26 (“the kings of the earth will bring their glory into” the New Jerusalem). Solomon’s Ophir gold is a foretaste of the nations’ ultimate tribute to the greater Solomon — not a commodity trade but a worship-rendering by all creation to the Lamb who sits on the eternal throne.
